title: Thermodynamic signatures of a field-induced ordered intermediate phase in Na$_2$Co$_2$TeO$_6$
authors: Prashanta K. Mukarjee, Sebastian Erdmann, R. Kalaivanan, R. Sankar, Kwang-Yong Choi, Alexander A. Tsirlin, Philipp Gegenwart
abstract: The honeycomb cobaltate Na$_2$Co$_2$TeO$_6$ has recently been proposed as a candidate material for hosting field-induced quantum spin liquid (QSL) behavior. Here, we present a comprehensive thermodynamic study of its low-temperature, high-field phase diagram using magnetization, specific heat, and magnetocaloric-effect measurements down to 1 K. In zero field, we observe a weak residual moment that provides further insight into the nature of the magnetic ground state. For in-plane magnetic fields ($B \parallel a^*$), we identify three field-induced transitions at $B_{c1} \simeq 6$ T, $B_{c2} \simeq 7.8$ T, and $B_{c3} \simeq 10.4$ T. The magnetic Grüneisen parameter and specific heat reveal clear thermodynamic signatures of these successive phase transitions enclosing two intermediate phases. Contrary to expectations for a field-induced QSL, the phase between $B_{c2}$ and $B_{c3}$ lacks enhanced magnetic entropy but instead shows behavior consistent with a distinct ordered state. Above $B_{c3}$, the absence of additional anomalies indicates a crossover to a conventional spin-polarized regime. Our results place stringent thermodynamic constraints on the proposed QSL scenario in Na$_2$Co$_2$TeO$_6$, calling for further microscopic investigations to establish the precise nature of the field-induced phases.
summary: This work presents a detailed thermodynamic investigation of $	ext{Na}_2	ext{Co}_2	ext{TeO}_6$ under high magnetic fields to probe its potential as a Quantum Spin Liquid. By measuring specific heat and magnetization, the authors identify three distinct field-induced phase transitions. Crucially, they argue that the observed thermodynamic behavior contradicts the expected signatures of a QSL in one of the intermediate phases.
main_problem: The study investigates the nature of field-induced ordered intermediate phases in the honeycomb cobaltate $	ext{Na}_2	ext{Co}_2	ext{TeO}_6$, specifically testing the hypothesis that it hosts Quantum Spin Liquid (QSL) behavior.
main_result: The thermodynamic measurements reveal successive field-induced transitions, but the phase between $B_{c2}$ and $B_{c3}$ lacks the expected enhanced magnetic entropy of a QSL, suggesting a distinct ordered state.
method: The research employed a comprehensive thermodynamic study using magnetization, specific heat, and magnetocaloric-effect measurements across a low-temperature, high-field phase diagram.
model_or_system: The physical system is the honeycomb cobaltate $	ext{Na}_2	ext{Co}_2	ext{TeO}_6$, which is studied as a candidate material for realizing QSL behavior, often compared to theoretical models like the Kitaev model.
key_observables: Magnetization ($M$), specific heat ($C_p$), magnetic Grüneisen parameter ($\Gamma_B$), and magnetic entropy ($\Delta S$).
important_parameters: In-plane magnetic fields ($B \parallel a^*$) at critical values $B_{c1} \simeq 6 	ext{ T}$, $B_{c2} \simeq 7.8 	ext{ T}$, and $B_{c3} \simeq 10.4 	ext{ T}$.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes that sharp anomalies in thermodynamic quantities correspond to well-defined phase transitions, and that the QSL state would exhibit specific entropy signatures.
figures_summary: Figures show $M$ and $C_p$ vs $T$ and $B$, and derivatives like $dM/dB$ and $d^2M/dB^2$, highlighting the three critical fields $B_{c1}, B_{c2}, B_{c3}$ through various anomalies.
paper_structure: The paper systematically presents thermodynamic measurements ($M, C_p, \Gamma_B$) across the field-temperature phase diagram, identifying three successive transitions and analyzing the thermodynamic signatures of the resulting intermediate phases.
why_interesting: The detailed thermodynamic mapping of field-induced transitions in a strongly correlated magnetic material like a cobaltate provides crucial experimental constraints for theoretical models of exotic quantum states, such as QSLs.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Magnetization and specific heat: (a) Zero-field cooled (ZFC) and field-cooled (FC) DC magnetic susceptibility versus T measured at B = 0.1 T. (b) Specific heat of NCTO in B = 0 T. The grey dotted lines indicate phase transitions. (c) Field-dependent magnetization, M(B), for NCTO at different temperatures down to 2 K. (d,e) The first and second derivative of M(B). The grey dotted lines indicate the positions of the critical fields.

FIG. 2. Residual moment: Temperature dependent magnetiza- tion measured in zero field after cooling the sample in weak train- ing fields for (a) in-plane (B ∥a∗) and (b) out-of-plane (B ∥c) directions.

FIG. 3. Field dependence of ΓB and specific heat: (a) Specific heat as a function of magnetic field at T = 2 K. (b) Magnetic Grüneisen parameter ΓB as a function of magnetic field at T = 2 K. (c) Field evolution of magnetic entropy at T = 2 K. (d) Field evolution of ΓB at different temperatures down to 1 K. The data are vertically offset for clarity. The gray lines indicate the position of the critical fields. The region between Bc2 and Bc3 is presented in yellowish-gray color. The data in this figure are shown for the increasing field-sweep cycle.

FIG. 4. Colour map: Colour map of the adiabatic MCE as a function of temperature T and magnetic field B for B ∥a∗. The color scale represents (dT/dB)S in units of K/T, where positive (yellow–red regions) and negative (blue regions) values highlight distinct field-induced regimes. The black lines display adiabatic temperature traces. Pronounced features near ∼8 T and ∼10– 11 T mark phase boundaries consistent with the critical fields dis- cussed in the text. The triangles and circles are from the position of broad maxima in Cp(B) and zero-crossings in ΓB which signal the appearance and disappearance of phase III as a function of field.

FIG. 5. B–T phase diagram of NCTO: To construct the mag- netic phase diagram, transition points were identified from charac- teristic features in thermodynamic and magnetic measurements. For temperature sweeps, peaks in d[χ(T)T]/dT and anomalies in the specific heat Cp(T) were used. For field sweeps, the phase boundaries were determined from peaks and broad minima in dM/dB and d2M/dB2, maxima in the field-dependent specific heat Cp(B), and zero crossings in the magnetic Grüneisen pa- rameter ΓB.
